sanskrit_devanagari:
उत्सीदेयुरिमे लोका न कुर्यां कर्म चेदहम् ।
सङ्करस्य च कर्ता स्यामुपहन्यामिमाः प्रजाः ॥ २४ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
utsīdeyur ime lokā
na kuryāṁ karma ced aham |
saṅkarasya ca kartā syām
upahanyām imāḥ prajāḥ || 24 ||

sanskrit_sources.agreement: exact (raw HTML scrape; akṣara sequence as published)
exact (text body identical with vedabase; punctuation rendering differs)
exact (text body identical; punctuation rendering differs)
exact (academic critical edition; cross-reference Bhg_03.024 = MBh_06,025.024). Body text identical; word-segmentation differs by edition.

translations.verbatim_quote: If I did not perform prescribed duties, all these worlds would be put to ruination. I would be the cause of creating unwanted population, and I would thereby destroy the peace of all living beings.
(See holy-bhagavad-gita.org for full Mukundananda rendering; agreement with Prabhupada noted in disagreements section where it diverges materially.)

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: Varṇa-saṅkara is unwanted population which disturbs the peace of the general society. In order to check this social disturbance, there are prescribed rules and regulations by which the population can automatically become peaceful and organized for spiritual progress in life.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 3.24 Cet, if; aham, I; na kuryam, do not perform; karma, action; all ime, these; lokah, worlds; utsideyuh, will be ruined, owing to the obsence of work responsible for the maintenance of the worlds.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara: utsīdeyur lokāḥ — the worlds would collapse — if I, the supreme actor, were not to perform action. I would become the cause of saṅkara — the confusion of the varṇas, the dissolution of the structured order in which functions are mapped to roles. And I would destroy these creatures — upahanyām imāḥ prajāḥ — by removing the model around which their order is built. The verse thus closes the loka-saṅgraha argument by naming the catastrophe that would result from its violation by the highest actor.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 3.24 If I do not do the work suitable to My station in life, likewise all the virtuous men also, neglecting their duties by following My example, would be destroyed on account of not performing their duties. That is, they will become lost.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja: the verse names the cosmic-order consequence. Were the Lord to disengage, the worlds would collapse — utsīdeyuḥ — because the order they participate in would lose its anchor. The Lord would become saṅkarasya kartā — the cause of confusion-of-classes — and would destroy the creatures by removing the model that gives their roles meaning. The verse is therefore the completion of the loka-saṅgraha argument: even the Lord's withdrawal is catastrophic, so no realised actor below has warrant for withdrawal.

positions.rendering: I would be the cause of creating unwanted population, and I would thereby destroy the peace of all living beings
I would be responsible for creating disorder in society, and would thus destroy the peace of the human race

saṅkara = varṇa-saṅkara, the confusion of the four classes that would result if their representatives ceased to perform their svadharma; this is a structural-order claim, not merely social-stability rhetoric
saṅkara = the dissolution of the structured order in which beings perform their proper functions; the Lord's disengagement would destroy not only the social order but the cosmic order it instances

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: Translators (especially modern devotional ones) often render saṅkara as 'unwanted population' or 'social disorder,' which captures the surface but loses both commentators' structural claim. For Shankara and Ramanuja the saṅkara is the collapse of the order in which roles map to functions; the verse is naming what fails when the top of the order stops modeling. Modern translations soften this by reading the verse sociologically; the commentators read it ontologically.

literal_meaning: These worlds would perish if I were not to perform action; I would be the cause of confusion-of-classes (saṅkara) and would destroy these creatures.
traditional_meaning_consensus: The completion of the loka-saṅgraha argument's consequence-side. Krishna names three consequences of his hypothetical disengagement: utsīdeyur lokāḥ (these worlds would collapse), saṅkarasya kartā syām (I would cause varṇa-saṅkara — class-mixing, social order disruption), upahanyām imāḥ prajāḥ (I would destroy these creatures). The verse is structurally important: it makes loka-saṅgraha not optional but causally load-bearing.
